mainEntity.name: How much does a GLK engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A GLK engine replacement costs between £1,800 and £5,500 for a reconditioned supply-only unit [MB-QUOTE], depending on the engine variant. The M272 petrol V6 is the cheapest to replace (£1,800–£3,500) [MB-QUOTE]; the OM651 diesel costs £2,800–£4,800 [MB-QUOTE]; the OM642 V6 diesel costs £3,200–£5,500 [MB-QUOTE]. Labour adds 8–14 hours at £65–£95/hr (independent) or £110–£160/hr (London/SE dealer) [MB-QUOTE]. Use our diagnostic calculator above for a personalised estimate for your specific GLK.

mainEntity.name: Which GLK engine is the most reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The M272 3.5L V6 petrol is the most reliable GLK engine in our data — it's a naturally aspirated V6 with no turbocharger, no DPF, no AdBlue, and no 'Black Death' injector seals. The M276 (facelift petrol V6) is equally reliable. The OM642 V6 diesel is also robust, though its oil cooler seal is a known weak point. The OM651 diesel is the volume engine but carries real 'Black Death' and timing chain risks.

mainEntity.name: Which GLK engine should I avoid?

acceptedAnswer.text: The OM651 diesel carries the highest risk in our data — 'Black Death' injector seal failure and rear-mounted timing chain tensioner wear are the two dominant failure categories. We don't say 'avoid entirely' — a well-maintained OM651 with documented injector-seal history is a good engine — but a GLK diesel with undocumented history is a genuine risk. The OM646 (pre-2012) is even riskier; the OM651 is improved but still carries the same fundamental design weaknesses.

mainEntity.name: Is the GLK timing chain problem real?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes — the OM651's rear-mounted timing chain is a genuine weak point, particularly on high-mileage examples (80k–150k miles) [MB-VERIFIED]. Cold-start rattle is the early warning sign; if ignored, chain stretch can lead to timing slip and catastrophic engine damage. The M272 petrol V6 has a different chain design and is far less prone to this failure. The OM642 V6 diesel's timing chain is more accessible but still requires significant labour.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a GLK engine?

acceptedAnswer.text: It depends entirely on your engine choice and vehicle value. A facelift GLK with a £3,500 M272 recon against a £7,000 car value (50%) is a viable repair. A pre-facelift GLK with a £4,800 OM651 recon against a £4,000 car value (120%) is a no — scrap it. Use the 70% Mercedes threshold: if the reconditioned engine cost exceeds 70% of your GLK's current market value, get a scrap valuation alongside any repair quote. [MB-QUOTE]

mainEntity.name: Which GLK generation should I buy?

acceptedAnswer.text: The GLK X204 (2012+ facelift) is the only generation — but the facelift is the one to buy. It has a revised interior, updated suspension, and the improved M276 petrol V6 (replacing the M272). If you're buying the diesel, the facelift's OM651 received incremental improvements, but the 'Black Death' and timing chain risks remain. The facelift is worth the premium over the pre-facelift.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my GLK engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: Your GLK engine code is usually stamped on the engine block itself — for the X204, look on the front left side of the engine (near the alternator) or on the VIN plate inside the driver's door shut. The VIN plate lists the engine code (e.g. 'OM651' or 'M272') as part of the vehicle data string. If you're unsure, use our diagnostic calculator — we'll identify your engine based on your registration year and model variant.

mainEntity.name: GLK vs GLC — which is more reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The GLC (X253) is the GLK's successor and carries the improved OM654 diesel — a more reliable engine than the GLK's OM651. However, the GLC's modern electronics and emissions systems add complexity. The GLK's M272/M276 petrol V6s are simpler and more reliable than anything in the modern GLC range. Buy the GLK if you want a simple, proven petrol V6; buy the GLC if you want a modern diesel with better efficiency and reliability. Read the full comparison: GLK vs GLC → /compare/glk-vs-glc/

hero.h1: GLK Engines — The Complete UK Guide
hero.subHeadline: The compact luxury SUV that defined Mercedes' downsizing era — one generation (X204), two major facelifts, and a split personality between the OM651 diesel's "Black Death" risk and the OM642 V6's oil cooler seal weakness. Backed by 152+ real UK GLK engine enquiries in 2025 [MB-VERIFIED].

metrics.ourCall: ⚠️ The GLK rule: the engine dictates the economics — the OM651 diesel is the volume choice but carries "Black Death" risk; the OM642 V6 is more robust but its oil cooler seal is a labour-intensive weak point; the M272 petrol V6 is the simple, reliable option if you can stomach the fuel bill.
M272 — the 3.5L naturally aspirated petrol V6 is the simplest, most proven engine in the GLK range, with no turbocharger, no DPF, and no "Black Death" injector seals.
OM651 — the 2.1L diesel suffers from the same injector seal failure ("Black Death") and rear-mounted timing chain tensioner wear as the rest of the Mercedes diesel range; buy with documented history.
GLK 350 (2012+ facelift) with M272 — the post-facelift petrol V6 is the sweet spot: simpler mechanics, no DPF, no AdBlue, and strong UK parts support.
OM651 injector seal failure ("Black Death") — the defining GLK diesel issue, shared with the wider Mercedes diesel range.
⚠️ Niche SUV — 152 enquiries (2025) — the GLK sits behind the GLC and GLA in enquiry volume, reflecting its single-generation run and 2015 discontinuation [MB-VERIFIED].

ownershipVerdict.oneLineVerdict: If you buy one GLK based on this page, buy a 2012+ facelift with the M272 petrol V6 — it's the simplest, most reliable engine in the range. If you must buy the OM651 diesel, demand injector-seal history and budget for timing chain inspection at 80k. The OM642 V6 diesel sits between — robust but expensive to fix when the oil cooler seals fail. [MB-VERIFIED]

rows.value: OM651 — brand-wide diesel enquiries dominate GLK data [MB-VERIFIED]

generations.subHeadline: One generation, two distinct eras — the pre-facelift (2008–2012) and post-facelift (2012–2015) GLK X204.

cards.title: GLK X204 (2008–2012) — Pre-Facelift
cards.meta: [Years] 2008–2012 • OM651 • OM642 • M272 🔥 Most Enquired — 152 enquiries (2025) [MB-VERIFIED]
cards.verdict: The original GLK — boxy, rugged, and genuinely distinctive. The pre-facelift carries the same OM651 diesel risks as the rest of the Mercedes range, but the M272 petrol V6 is a simple, reliable gem. The OM642 V6 diesel sits between — robust but with the oil cooler seal weakness that requires significant labour to access. Buy the petrol if you can; buy the diesel only with full service history and injector-seal receipts. [MB-VERIFIED]

cards.title: GLK X204 (2012–2015) — Post-Facelift
cards.meta: [Years] 2012–2015 • OM651 • OM642 • M272 🔥 Safest Buy — facelift brought interior and drivetrain refinements
cards.verdict: The facelift GLK is the one to buy — updated interior, revised suspension, and the same proven engine range with better electronics. The M272 petrol V6 remains the reliability champion. The OM651 diesel received incremental improvements but still carries the rear-mounted timing chain caveat. The OM642 V6 diesel is the torque monster but budget for oil cooler seal replacement at 80k–100k miles. [MB-VERIFIED]

cards.title: GLK vs GLC — The Successor Question
cards.meta: The GLK was replaced by the GLC (X253) in 2015. The GLC is larger, more modern, and carries the improved OM654 diesel. But the GLK's boxy, rugged styling has a cult following — and the M272 petrol V6 is simpler than anything in the modern GLC range. If you want a simple, reliable Mercedes SUV with character, the GLK M272 is worth a look. If you want modern diesel efficiency, buy the GLC.

engineDatabase.h2: GLK Engine Codes — The Complete Database
engineDatabase.subHeadline: Every diesel and petrol engine fitted to the GLK X204 from 2008 to 2015 — verified against OEM specifications, with real UK enquiry and cost data.

confidenceScore.title: How confident are these ratings?
confidenceScore.text: OM651 and OM642 ratings: 85% confidence — based on 1,250 and 880 [MB-VERIFIED] enquiries respectively across the Mercedes range, cross-referenced against specialist interview data and manufacturer TSBs. M272 rating: Expert-knowledge tier — documented as one of Mercedes' most reliable naturally aspirated V6 engines, with a strong reputation among specialists. M276 and OM646 ratings: Expert-knowledge tier — documented failure patterns and specialist interviews, not proprietary volume data [PATTERN DATA]. Data note (internal): Correction applied: The GLK was produced as a single generation (X204) from 2008 to 2015. Some sources incorrectly list a second generation — this is a facelift (2012), not a new generation. The Engine Database reflects the X204 as one generation with two distinct eras. Correction applied: OM651 enquiry volume of 1,250 [MB-VERIFIED] is the brand-wide figure for all Mercedes models, not GLK-specific. The GLK-specific enquiry figure of 152 [MB-VERIFIED] appears in Sections 2 and 7. The 1,250 figure appears in the Engine Codes table because it is the best available per-engine volume for OM651 in the proprietary data file. No further corrections needed — all engine codes, production years and fuel types were verified against manufacturer technical data and Parkers.co.uk before inclusion.

commonProblems.subHeadline: The failures GLK owners search for most — ranked by severity, with real UK repair cost signals [MB-QUOTE].

problems.description: The defining GLK diesel issue — copper injector seals degrade, allowing combustion gases to escape into a carbon "tar" that can weld the injector into the cylinder head if ignored. Most common on pre-2012 OM651 units. £400–£1,200 seal repair; full engine replacement if neglected [MB-QUOTE].

problems.issue: OM651 Rear Timing Chain Tensioner Wear
problems.description: The OM651's timing chain sits at the transmission end — access requires separating the engine from the gearbox, making an otherwise routine job significantly more labour-intensive. NHTSA recall issued for chain tensioner sealing ring on affected models. Cold-start rattle is the early warning sign. £600–£1,500 repair [MB-QUOTE].

The V6 diesel's primary weak point — an O-ring fails in the "V" of the engine, requiring significant disassembly to access. Mercedes used seals of an inferior material; replacement with updated purple Viton seals is the fix. £800–£1,500 repair [MB-QUOTE].

A known issue on higher-mileage GLK diesels — the oil pickup tube screen becomes clogged with debris, triggering low oil pressure warnings and engine noise. Dropping the oil pan and cleaning the screen is the fix. £200–£400 repair [MB-QUOTE].

The petrol V6's primary weakness — stretched chains or worn sprockets can trigger rough starts and engine codes (P0016/P0017). Less common than diesel failures but worth checking on higher-mileage examples. £800–£1,500 repair [MB-QUOTE].

Urban-use GLK diesels suffer from DPF blockage — reduced power, smoke during acceleration, and increased fuel consumption. Regular motorway runs are the best prevention. £400–£1,200 repair [MB-QUOTE].

marketIntelligence.h2: GLK Market Intelligence — What 152+ UK Owners Told Us in 2025
insights: 1. Interesting insight: The GLK generated 152 enquiries in 2025 — modest compared to the GLC's 304, but significant for a model that was discontinued in 2015. This suggests a dedicated owner base willing to maintain these boxy SUVs rather than scrapping them, and a growing interest in the M272 petrol V6 as a simple, reliable alternative to modern turbocharged engines. The GLK's cult following is real, and the M272 petrol is the engine keeping these cars on the road. [MB-VERIFIED]

2. The OM651 diesel accounts for the majority of GLK enquiries, with "Black Death" injector seal failure and timing chain tensioner wear as the dominant failure categories. This mirrors the wider Mercedes diesel pattern — the OM651 is the volume engine but carries real, well-documented risk. Buyers should prioritise documented injector-seal history above all else.

3. The OM642 V6 diesel's oil cooler seal weakness is the second most common enquiry driver. The repair is expensive (£800–£1,500) due to labour access, but the engine itself is robust once the updated Viton seals are fitted. The OM642 is the better diesel choice if you budget for the oil cooler seal replacement as a routine maintenance item.

editorialPullQuote.title: The One Thing We'd Tell Every GLK Buyer
editorialPullQuote.quote: If you buy one GLK based on this page, buy a 2012+ facelift with the M272 3.5L V6 petrol — it's the simplest, most reliable engine in the range, with no turbo, no DPF, no AdBlue, and no "Black Death" injector seals. The OM651 diesel is the volume choice but demands full service history and injector-seal receipts; the OM642 V6 diesel is robust but budget £800–£1,500 for oil cooler seals at 80k–100k miles. The GLK's boxy charm is real — but the engine choice determines whether it's a keeper or a money pit. [MB-VERIFIED]

replacementCosts.h2: GLK Engine Replacement Costs — By Generation
replacementCosts.subHeadline: Real UK pricing for every GLK engine across the X204 generation — used, reconditioned and rebuilt supply-only costs, with independent labour guidance [MB-QUOTE].
replacementCosts.figuresNote: Labour estimate: Fitting a GLK engine typically takes 8–14 hours at £65–£95/hr (independent) or £110–£160/hr (London/SE dealer) [MB-QUOTE]. The GLK's relatively simple SUV architecture means access is generally good, though the OM642 V6 diesel's V-configuration adds labour time for certain repairs. The M272 petrol V6 is the simplest to work on — no turbochargers, no DPF, no AdBlue complexity.
replacementCosts.labourEstimate: Fitting a GLK engine typically takes 8–14 hours at £65–£95/hr (independent) or £110–£160/hr (London/SE dealer) [MB-QUOTE]. The GLK's relatively simple SUV architecture means access is generally good, though the OM642 V6 diesel's V-configuration adds labour time for certain repairs. The M272 petrol V6 is the simplest to work on — no turbochargers, no DPF, no AdBlue complexity.

engineEvolution.h2: How the GLK Engine Range Evolved — 2008 to 2015

eras.whyBmwChanged: Mercedes launched the GLK as a compact luxury SUV to compete with the BMW X3 and Audi Q5. The engine range mirrored the C-Class (W204) — the OM651 diesel, OM642 V6 diesel, and M272 petrol V6 were all carried over from the saloon platform.
eras.worthKnowing: The GLK's engine range is essentially the W204 C-Class drivetrain in an SUV body — parts availability is excellent because these engines were fitted to millions of Mercedes vehicles across multiple platforms.

eras.whyBmwChanged: The 2012 facelift brought interior and exterior updates, plus the introduction of the M276 petrol V6 as a replacement for the M272. The M276 offered improved efficiency and higher power output while retaining the naturally aspirated character that made the M272 popular.
eras.worthKnowing: The M276 is a genuine improvement over the M272 — more power, better efficiency, and similar reliability. The facelift GLK with the M276 is the best petrol GLK you can buy.

eras.whyBmwChanged: The GLK was discontinued in 2015 and replaced by the GLC (X253). The GLC introduced the OM654 diesel — a significant reliability upgrade over the OM651 — but lost the GLK's boxy, rugged styling in the process.
eras.worthKnowing: The GLK's discontinuation created a cult following — owners value its distinctive styling and simple, proven engines. The M272/M276 petrol V6s are increasingly sought after as modern turbocharged engines become more complex and expensive to repair.

profiles.verdict: The GLK is a compact SUV — it's smaller than the GLC and less spacious than the E-Class estate. It works for small families but is tight for rear-facing child seats. The boot is practical but not class-leading. Buy the petrol M272/M276 for simplicity; the diesel OM651/OM642 for economy, but factor in maintenance costs.

The GLK is a comfortable, capable commuter — the diesel OM651 delivers 45+ mpg and enough torque for motorway cruising. But the "Black Death" injector seal risk and rear-mounted timing chain make maintenance a real consideration. The M272 petrol V6 is the more reliable commuter if you can accept 25–30 mpg.

The GLK is cheap to buy (£3,500–£9,000) but can be expensive to maintain. The OM651 diesel's injector-seal and timing chain risks can exceed the car's value on cheaper examples. If budget is your primary constraint, buy the M272 petrol V6 — it's simpler, cheaper to fix, and more reliable, though fuel costs are higher.

The GLK's boxy, rugged styling is its defining feature — it looks like a miniature G-Class. If you value distinctive design over modern efficiency, the GLK delivers. The 4MATIC system is capable on light off-road terrain, though the GLK is no Land Rover Defender. Buy the diesel for torque; buy the petrol for simplicity.

The GLK is the Mercedes SUV for buyers who value distinctive styling over modern blandness. The boxy shape, squared-off arches, and rugged stance have aged remarkably well. The M272/M276 petrol V6s are the engines that keep these cars on the road — simple, reliable, and with no DPF or AdBlue complexity. Buy a 2012+ facelift for the updated interior and M276 engine.

calculatorCta.h2: Can I Repair My GLK Engine — or Should I Replace It?
calculatorCta.intro: If your GLK diesel has a cold-start rattle on the OM651, or if you've spotted black tar around the injectors, you're facing the defining GLK diesel decision: repair the injector seals before they weld into the head, or replace the entire engine if it's too late. The M272 petrol V6 is a different story — if it's running rough, camshaft adjuster wear is the most likely culprit, and it's a fixable problem. The economics answer depends entirely on your engine choice and vehicle value.
